us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With War as Hormuz Tensions Escalate
us-politics.body:
Trump posted an Easter message threatening Iran with 'Hell' if it blocks the Strait of Hormuz, and declined to rule out ground troops if nuclear talks fail. Netanyahu publicly congratulated Trump after a US mission rescued a downed pilot, while Iran confirmed US aircraft were destroyed during a separate recovery operation for a missing service member. The incidents mark a sharp escalation in direct US-Iran military contact.

Opinion is fractured across party lines - and within them. Marjorie Taylor Greene called Trump's threats 'insane.' Top Armed Services Democrat Adam Smith warned a war would be a costly mistake. One House Republican said boots on the ground would only be justified to seize enriched uranium. A former Trump adviser said the aggressive rhetoric makes a negotiated deal less likely, not more.

sources.title: Netanyahu says he spoke with Trump, congratulated him on rescuing downed pilot
Iran says US aircraft destroyed during recovery mission for missing service member
House Republican says he doesn’t think ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz
Marjorie Taylor Greene rails against ‘insane’ Trump after Easter Iran threat
Top Armed Services Democrat says Iran war ‘mistake in large part because of the cost’
Trump declines to rule out ground troops if Iran doesn’t make a deal
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
House Republican: ‘Only purpose’ for US boots on the ground in Iran is ‘to get the enriched uranium’
Former Trump adviser on president’s latest Iran threat: ‘Not going to get a deal’
House Democrat: Iran’s control of Strait of Hormuz ‘more strategically vital’ than nuclear weapon

us-politics.headline: DHS Shutdown Drags On as Congress Stays on Recess
us-politics.body:
The Department of Homeland Security remains unfunded after Congress failed to pass a spending bill before recess. Republican divisions and what sources describe as Trump's detachment from the process have stalled efforts to reopen the agency. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries called on Speaker Mike Johnson to cut the recess short and act.

The standoff reflects broader dysfunction in Republican budget negotiations. Trump separately said he does not expect further Cabinet shake-ups following the firings of Pam Bondi and Kristi Noem, though Senator Tim Kaine said Bondi's dismissal should serve as a warning to whoever takes the attorney general role next.

sources.title: Republican divisions, Trump’s detachment stymie GOP efforts to reopen DHS
Trump says to not expect Cabinet shake-up after Bondi, Noem firings
Jeffries calls on Johnson to end House recess amid DHS funding lapse
Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee

us-politics.headline: Democrats Eye 2028 as Party Debates Strategy and Messaging
us-politics.body:
Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego said he is open to a 2028 presidential run, telling reporters 'we have to look at it.' Representative Ro Khanna argued Democrats must engage with non-traditional media figures like streamer Hasan Piker or risk losing younger voters in future elections. The comments reflect a live internal debate about how the party rebuilds after 2024.

A separate opinion piece warned that Democrats' focus on affordability as a campaign theme could backfire if voters do not credit the party with delivering results. Maryland Governor Wes Moore added his voice to the economic debate, arguing that ending foreign wars is the most direct path to lowering gas prices.

sources.title: Moore: Ending foreign wars ‘best thing that we could do’ to lower gas prices
Khanna: Those saying ‘don’t engage’ with personalities like Hasan Piker ‘will cost us future elections’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’
‘Affordability’ could eventually bite Democrats in the bum

us-politics.headline: Pope Leo Calls for Peace; UCLA Wins First Women's Title
us-politics.body:
Pope Leo used his Easter address to deliver a message of peace directed at a world he described as consumed by war, drawing wide attention given the ongoing US-Iran conflict. The address was seen as an implicit rebuke of current Western military postures, though the Vatican stopped short of naming any government directly.

On a lighter note, UCLA won its first NCAA women's basketball championship. Both Barack Obama and California Governor Gavin Newsom publicly congratulated the Bruins, giving the moment a political edge given Newsom's widely discussed 2028 ambitions.

sources.title: Obama, Newsom congratulate UCLA for winning first NCAA women’s basketball championship
On Easter, Pope Leo delivers commanding message of peace to a world at war

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Bites as Hormuz Standoff Drags Into May
au-politics.body: Energy Minister Chris Bowen confirmed fuel shipments to Australia are locked in only until May, as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed amid escalating US-Iran tensions. Bowen has publicly called for the strait's reopening, while Donald Trump issued fresh expletive-laden threats against Iran, with analysts warning his stated timeframes carry little credibility. The crisis is reshaping consumer behaviour at home. Petrol station owners report severe margin pressure, some questioning whether their businesses remain viable. Used electric vehicle prices are rising sharply, and e-bike retailers say sales are surging as commuters seek alternatives to soaring fuel costs.

sources.title: Australia news live: BoM says cyclone Maila may approach Queensland; Chris Bowen calls for Hormuz reopening
Trump's latest tirade shows timeframes 'all but meaningless'
Live: Trump's Iran threat in expletive-laden post; Bowen says fuel shipments are now locked 'into May'
Price crunch has some servo owners asking: 'Is it really worth doing this?'
Commuters are switching from cars to e-bikes to save money on fuel
‘Doesn’t make sense to hold on to a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits

au-politics.headline: Moira Deeming Wins Preselection in Victorian Liberal Battle
au-politics.body: Moira Deeming has secured Victorian Liberal preselection, ending a prolonged internal dispute over her place in the party. Deeming was previously stripped of the Liberal whip by then-leader John Pesutto following her attendance at a gender-critical rally linked to far-right figures. Her return signals a factional shift within the Victorian Liberals ahead of the next state election. The outcome deepens existing tensions between the party's moderate and conservative wings, with no clear resolution to the ideological divisions her case exposed.

sources.title: Australia news live: BoM says cyclone Maila may approach Queensland; Chris Bowen calls for Hormuz reopening
Moira Deeming wins Victorian Liberal preselection battle

au-politics.headline: Racial Discrimination Allegations Put NSW Government Under Scrutiny
au-politics.body: The Australian Human Rights Commission has launched an investigation into the NSW government over allegations of racial discrimination. The probe adds pressure to the Minns government as it navigates a range of social policy challenges. Separately, a family court ruled that an Aboriginal child relocated 1,700 kilometres from their remote Northern Territory community must be returned, finding the move unjustified. The two cases together highlight ongoing legal and institutional scrutiny of how governments at multiple levels treat First Nations people.

sources.title: Australian Human Rights Commission investigates NSW government over allegations of racial discrimination
Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from remote NT community should be returned, family court rules

au-politics.headline: Cyclone Maila Threatens Queensland Coast Over Easter
au-politics.body: The Bureau of Meteorology is tracking Tropical Cyclone Maila as it approaches the Queensland coast, bringing scattered rain and strong winds to eastern parts of the state. Forecasters are monitoring its intensity closely over the Easter long weekend, when coastal tourism typically peaks. Fire-affected communities in Victoria are separately welcoming Easter visitors as part of their economic recovery, while a mass die-off of baby flying foxes in the Northern Territory - caused by Cyclone Narelle - has left wildlife carers overwhelmed.

sources.title: Australia news live: BoM says cyclone Maila may approach Queensland; Chris Bowen calls for Hormuz reopening
Scattered rain, strong wind along Qld coast as forecasters watch TC Maila
Fire-affected towns welcome Easter visitors
Vet helping native bat pups has 'never seen mass casualties like this'

world.headline: Iran War Escalates as Hormuz Standoff Reaches Breaking Point
world.body:
Tehran has rejected Trump's Tuesday deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, defying an expletive-laden social media threat from the president that warned of severe consequences. The standoff is pushing global oil markets to the edge - BBC reporters near the strait describe the area as feeling like the edge of a battlefield. OPEC+ has moved to hike output, but warned of a slow recovery from damage caused by strikes on regional infrastructure.

The US claimed a win after rescuing a downed American airman from a remote part of Iran, with Trump declaring victory. But military threats to the operation remain active, and Iran's wartime internet shutdown - now the longest on record - signals the regime is digging in. Israel has struck targets in both Iran and Lebanon simultaneously, killing at least 14 in Lebanon and drawing Iranian missile fire onto Haifa and southern Israel.

sources.title: Trump declares victory after rescue but threats to US operation in Iran still loom
Trump issues expletive-laden threat to Iran over Hormuz Strait blockage
Iran war live: Tehran rejects Trump’s Tuesday deadline on Strait of Hormuz
'We are at the edge of a battlefield': BBC reports near Strait of Hormuz
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded
Trump threatens Iran in expletive-filled social media post
Frustration grows as Iran’s wartime internet shutdown breaks grim record
Video captures Iranian missile striking residential building in Haifa
OPEC+ agrees to hike oil output, warns of slow recovery after attacks
Iranian missile strikes industrial area in southern Israel

world.headline: Gaza Deteriorates as Hamas Refuses Disarmament and Strikes Continue
world.body:
Hamas's armed wing has flatly rejected Israeli and US demands for disarmament, calling them unacceptable, while Israeli air strikes continue to kill civilians across Gaza. One strike hit a crowd in Gaza City. Inside the territory, tent camps are now plagued by rodent infestations, and Christians marked Easter this year in mourning rather than celebration.

The humanitarian situation is drawing renewed criticism, with questions mounting over whether the international community has effectively abandoned Gaza's population. Israel is conducting simultaneous military operations in Gaza, Lebanon, and Iran - a three-front campaign that is stretching regional stability and drawing little coordinated international response.

sources.title: Has the humanitarian crisis in Gaza been ignored?
Hamas armed wing says disarmament demands not acceptable
Israeli air strike hits crowd in Gaza City
Hamas armed wing rejects disarmament while Israel continues attacks
Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Rodent infestations and attacks spread in Gaza tent camps
‘Refuse to celebrate’: Christians in Gaza mark sombre Easter amid genocide

world.headline: Explosives Found at Russian Gas Pipeline Spanning Serbia and Hungary
world.body:
Serbia and Hungary have both confirmed that explosives were discovered planted at a Russian gas pipeline running through their territories. Hungary framed the find as a deliberate plot timed ahead of its upcoming election, raising immediate questions about sabotage and political interference. No group has claimed responsibility.

The incident adds fresh tension to an already volatile European energy landscape. It follows a pattern of infrastructure attacks across the continent and will intensify scrutiny of pipeline security at a moment when several governments remain dependent on Russian gas flows.

sources.title: Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Serbia, Hungary say explosives found at Russian gas pipeline

world.headline: Lebanon Displacement Deepens as Nigeria Rescues Church Hostages
world.body:
Ongoing Israeli strikes across Lebanon have killed at least 14 people in the past 24 hours, adding to a displacement crisis that has already uprooted hundreds of thousands. Residents describe lives turned upside down, with little clarity on when or whether they can return home.

Elsewhere, Nigeria's army rescued 31 hostages seized during an attack on a church, a rare success against armed groups that have terrorised communities across the country's middle belt. In Southeast Asia, Vietnam's gig workers are absorbing the economic shockwaves of the Iran conflict, with surging fuel costs squeezing delivery drivers and ride-hailing workers who have no buffer against rising prices.

sources.title: Vietnam’s gig workers slammed by rising fuel costs amid fallout of Iran war
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Nigeria's army rescues 31 hostages after church attack
Israeli air strikes, shelling across Lebanon add to rising death toll
At least 14 people killed in Israeli strikes across Lebanon

ai.headline: Investors Flee OpenAI as Anthropic Momentum Builds
ai.body:
OpenAI is losing its grip on investor confidence, with capital flowing toward Anthropic as concerns mount about leadership, product direction, and Sam Altman's expanding ambitions. The shift marks a striking reversal for a company that dominated AI headlines just two years ago.

Meanwhile, Microsoft's Copilot - built on OpenAI's models - quietly updated its terms of service to describe the product as "for entertainment purposes only," a legal hedge that raises real questions about enterprise reliability. Competitors are moving fast. Google's Gemma 4 is now running on iPhones and locally via LM Studio, and developers are already benchmarking it against production workloads.

sources.title: OpenAI's fall from grace as investors race to Anthropic
Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use
Gemma 4 on iPhone
Running Gemma 4 locally with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code

ai.headline: Japan's Labor Shortage Is Robotics' Best Proving Ground
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots not to displace workers but to fill roles that go unfilled - care facilities, logistics, and agriculture. The country's shrinking workforce has created conditions where experimental hardware gets real-world testing at scale, accelerating the feedback loop between lab and deployment.

The trend extends beyond humanoids. Peter Thiel is backing solar-powered GPS collars for cattle, a bet that precision livestock management is ready for mainstream adoption. Both cases point to the same pattern: labor scarcity is driving adoption of physical AI faster than any product pitch could.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn't coming for your job; it's filling the one nobody wants
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants
Peter Thiel's big bet on solar-powered cow collars

ai.headline: AI Music, Copyright, and Suno's Growing Legal Exposure
ai.body:
Suno's AI music generator is deepening its copyright entanglement, with The Verge reporting that the platform's outputs closely mimic real artists in ways that existing licensing frameworks were never built to handle. Lawsuits are already in motion, and the music industry is watching closely.

The broader tension is familiar - AI tools trained on copyrighted material generating commercial output - but music makes it visceral. Suno's covers are recognizable enough to confuse listeners. That's the legal problem. Courts will have to decide whether similarity is enough, and the answer will shape every generative audio platform that follows.

sources.title: Suno is a music copyright nightmare

ai.headline: Employers Are Mining Your Data to Lowball Your Salary
ai.body:
Companies are using personal data - browsing history, location patterns, financial signals, and social profiles - to model the minimum salary a candidate will accept before making an offer. The practice is spreading quietly through HR software vendors who package it as "compensation intelligence."

Workers have almost no visibility into this process and limited legal recourse in most jurisdictions. The asymmetry is stark: employers arrive at negotiations with a data-derived floor, while candidates guess. It is a structural advantage built on the same data-brokerage ecosystem that powers targeted advertising, now pointed directly at the labor market.

sources.title: Employers use your personal data to figure out the lowest salary you'll accept

btc.headline: Polymarket Pulls Iran Markets After Backlash, Odds Hit 63%
btc.body:
Prediction market Polymarket removed a set of Iran-related betting markets after users pushed back hard against their framing. The controversy erupted after Trump posted about Iran, sending odds of a US invasion this year to 63% on the platform.

The pulled markets had asked users to bet on scenarios framed around an Iranian rescue operation, which critics called exploitative and irresponsible. Polymarket did not immediately explain the specific criteria that triggered the removal. The episode highlights a recurring tension for prediction markets - how to handle politically sensitive events without crossing ethical lines.

sources.title: Polymarket odds of US invading Iran this year reach 63% after Trump's post
Polymarket pulls controversial Iran rescue markets after intense backlash

btc.headline: Bitcoin Fronts the Fed as ETF Money Rewires Market Behavior
btc.body:
Bitcoin is no longer reacting to Federal Reserve signals - it is moving ahead of them. Analysts point to the flood of institutional capital through spot ETFs as the driver, arguing that professional traders now position in bitcoin before macro announcements rather than after.

A Bitcoin Policy Institute executive added another layer, describing bitcoin and the US dollar as having a symbiotic rather than adversarial relationship. That framing cuts against the narrative that bitcoin is purely a dollar hedge. Together, the two arguments suggest bitcoin's market structure has matured significantly, behaving less like a speculative asset and more like a macro instrument.

sources.title: Bitcoin and the US dollar have a 'symbiotic' relationship: BPI exec
Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause

btc.headline: Quantum Computing and AI Tighten the Crypto Security Vise
btc.body:
Two separate threats to crypto security landed in the same news cycle. Ledger's CTO warned that AI is accelerating attacks on crypto users - making phishing, social engineering, and exploit development faster and cheaper for bad actors.

Separately, a CoinDesk explainer laid out why quantum computing poses an existential risk to Bitcoin specifically. Quantum machines, once powerful enough, could theoretically break the elliptic curve cryptography that secures Bitcoin wallets. Neither threat is immediate, but both are moving faster than most of the industry is prepared for.

sources.title: A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin
AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns

btc.headline: Drift Incident Could Trigger Civil Negligence Claims, Lawyer Says
btc.body:
A crypto attorney says the recent Drift protocol incident may meet the legal threshold for civil negligence, raising the prospect of user lawsuits against the platform. The key question is whether Drift owed users a duty of care and failed to meet it.

If courts agree, it could set a precedent that DeFi protocols are not shielded from liability simply because they operate on-chain. That would be a significant shift. Most DeFi projects have operated under the assumption that decentralization insulates them from legal exposure.

sources.title: Crypto attorney says Drift incident may qualify as 'civil negligence'

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines broiled until silky and caramelised under a sweet-savoury miso and mirin glaze
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before glazing so the miso penetrates deeply rather than just sitting on the surface

exercise.name: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or heavy shopping bags, a clear 10-metre corridor or garden path
exercise.description: Cycle through farmer carries, suitcase carries, and overhead carries for timed sets, building grip strength, core stability, and real-world functional fitness in a way most gym routines completely ignore

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had every component replaced remains fundamentally the same thing — and philosophers from Hobbes to Parfit have weaponised it to interrogate whether you are the same person you were ten years ago
learn.why: On a Monday at the start of a new week it is a surprisingly practical rabbit hole: Derek Parfit's modern extension into psychological continuity theory will quietly reframe how you think about habits, self-improvement, and why you keep breaking promises to your past self
